name: Do I really need a written contract with a general contractor, and what should it include?
headline: Do I really need a written contract with a general contractor, and what should it include?

mainEntity.name: Do I really need a written contract with a general contractor, and what should it include?
mainEntity.text: Do I really need a written contract with a general contractor, and what should it include?

acceptedAnswer.text: A written contract protects both you and the contractor. For any real project in Pacifica, Daly City, San Bruno, Hillsborough, or Half Moon Bay, you should absolutely insist on a clear, detailed contract before work starts. At minimum, it should include: - Contractor’s legal name, license number, and contact info - Your name and project address - Detailed scope of work in plain language - List of materials and finishes (or allowances) - Total price and payment schedule - Start date and an estimated completion date - How change orders and extra costs are handled - Who gets permits and schedules inspections - Warranty terms on labor and materials Read every line, and ask about anything you don’t understand. Don’t accept verbal promises like “we’ll take care of that” without seeing them in writing. Never sign a blank contract or one with big sections labeled “TBD” unless you’re comfortable that those items will be handled as change orders later. Also confirm how disputes are handled and what happens if either side wants to stop the project early. If something in the contract makes you uncomfortable, ask the contractor to revise it. A professional should not pressure you to sign quickly or be offended by reasonable questions.
